does: This paper presents a detailed policy proposal for structuring U.S. federal governance of frontier artificial intelligence systems. The blueprint proposes institutional mechanisms for oversight, safety evaluation, and security assessment of advanced AI systems, with mechanisms to enforce standards across developers. It addresses licensing frameworks for AI systems that meet certain capability thresholds, requirements for safety testing and red-teaming before deployment, and coordination structures between government agencies responsible for AI governance. The proposal attempts to balance innovation and competitive concerns against safety and national security objectives through a centralized regulatory approach.
connects: This work builds on emerging policy discussions around AI governance that have intensified alongside rapid advances in large language models and frontier AI capabilities. It responds to prior work examining AI safety standards, government oversight models from other technological domains like nuclear energy and aviation, and earlier governance proposals from policy institutions and safety researchers. The blueprint draws on established regulatory frameworks while incorporating lessons from discussions about AI alignment, interpretability requirements, and the need for safety evaluation before deployment, synthesizing these into a concrete institutional proposal for federal-level coordination.
enables: This proposal creates a foundation for subsequent policy development and implementation of federal AI governance structures. It enables future work on specific technical standards for safety certification, detailed mechanisms for capability assessment thresholds that trigger regulatory oversight, and coordination protocols between different government agencies. The blueprint also supports development of international governance frameworks by providing a model that other countries might adapt or coordinate with. It points toward future work on defining concrete safety requirements, establishing expertise within government institutions, and building the technical and procedural infrastructure needed to evaluate and approve frontier AI systems at scale.
